note: Pilot cleanup projects (Philippines). Priority = Severity × Exposure × Ecology (each 1–5) → 0–100. Severity for Manila Bay uses our Lebreton river data (Pasig is a top global emitter); Exposure and Ecology are curated pilot estimates and are clearly illustrative. Cost = goal tonnes × local $0.50–$2.50/kg cleanup (modeled, banded — NOT a guarantee).

projects.name: Manila Bay & the Pasig River
projects.plastic_note: The Pasig is among the world's highest-emitting plastic rivers (~41k t/yr in our data); its load lands in Manila Bay.
projects.why: A megacity river-to-bay pathway: extreme plastic load, ~13M people, and a bay with mangroves and migratory-bird habitat.

projects.name: Malapascua & northern Cebu
projects.plastic_note: Island and nearshore plastic accumulation on fishing and dive-tourism coasts (illustrative).
projects.why: World-renowned thresher-shark and reef diving plus fishing communities — high ecological and livelihood stakes.

projects.name: Siargao Island
projects.plastic_note: Beach and jungle plastic on a surf-tourism island with reefs and mangrove sanctuaries (illustrative).
projects.why: A protected-reef surf island where tourism, mangroves, and a local recycling economy all hinge on clean coasts.

projects.name: Negros Oriental coast (Zamboanguita)
projects.plastic_note: Nearshore and beach plastic along reef and seagrass coasts (illustrative).
projects.why: Reef and seagrass coasts near Apo Island with a full-time local cleanup team doing daily removals.

projects.name: Stop it upstream — national prevention
projects.why: Cleanup is downstream. Preventing plastic from leaking in the first place — better waste systems, refill, brand accountability, policy — is usually far more cost-effective per tonne.
